The theory of relativity, developed by Albert Einstein, comprises two interrelated parts: special relativity, which describes how the laws of physics are the same for all non‑accelerating observers and shows that space and time are linked into spacetime, and general relativity, which explains gravity as the curvature of spacetime caused by mass and energy.

The theory of relativity, formulated by Albert Einstein, describes how space and time are interwoven into a single continuum and how gravity results from the curvature of that spacetime. It comprises special relativity, which addresses the behavior of objects at constant high velocities, and general relativity, which explains gravitational effects as the warping of spacetime by mass and energy.

The theory of relativity, developed by Albert Einstein, comprises two interrelated parts: special relativity, which shows that the laws of physics are the same for all inertial observers and that space and time are interwoven into a single spacetime continuum, and general relativity, which extends this to accelerated frames and describes gravity as the curvature of spacetime caused by mass‑energy.
